coredata.dc:title: Turn on fluorescence detection of curcumin in food matrices by the novel fluorescence sensitizer
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ya-Qian
Li, Li
Yin, Jin
Yu, Xu
Wu, Xiaowei
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: In this study, silane reagents were for the first time explored as the fluorescence sensitizer. They were demonstrated to have fluorescence sensitization effect on curcumin and 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) possessed the strongest effect. Thus, GPTMS was adopted as the novel fluorescence sensitizer to turn on the fluorescence of curcumin by more than two orders of magnitude for detection. In this way, curcumin could be determined with a linear range of 0.2–2000 ng/mL and an LOD of 0.067 ng/mL. The method was applicable to determine curcumin in several actual food samples, which had the good consistency with the high performance liquid chromatographic method, demonstrating the high accuracy of the proposed method. In addition, the curcumins sensitized by GPTMS could be cured under certain conditions and held the potential for solid fluorescence application. This study expanded the scope of fluorescence sensitizer to silane reagents, and provided the novel approach for fluorescence detection of curcumin and further to generate new solid fluorescence system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensitizer
Silane reagent
Curcumin
Solid-state fluorescence

The fluorescence sensitization of curcumin by silane reagents

Quantification of curcumin using GPTMS as fluorescence sensitizer
Quantification of curcumin in genuine food samples
Solid-state fluorescence of curcumin sensitized by GPTMS
Results and discussions
Sensitized fluorescence of curcumin by various silane reagents
Optical properties of curcumin in GPTMS
Mechanism of fluorescence sensitization of curcumin by GPTMS
Quantification of the curcumin using GPTMS as the fluorescence sensitizer
Fluorescence detection of curcumin in genuine food samples using GPTMS as the fluorescence sensitizer
Comparison of detection methods for curcumin
Solid-state fluorescence of curcumin sensitized by GPTMS
Conclusion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering (SKLBEE2021014). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

The authors acknowledge the financial support of Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering ( SKLBEE2021014 ). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence sensitization effects of different silane reagents on curcumin (18 μg/mL). Insert: photos of curcumin in different silane reagents under natural light (up) and UV light (down) (λ=365 nm) (0 was blank GPTMS, 10 was saturated aqueous solution of curcumin (the solubility of curcumin at the room temperature is smaller than 18 μg/mL); and 1–9 were consistent with the abscissa of the figure from left to right).

The fluorescence intensity of curcumin of different concentrations in GPTMS at the respective λmax ex/λmax em and (B) the respective λmax ex/λmax em normalized fluorescence spectra. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of GPTMS, curcumin saturated aqueous solution, 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS (λex = 377 nm). Insert: Photographs of ①GPTMS, ②18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS and ③curcumin saturated aqueous solution in natural light and UV light (λ = 365 nm) in a dark box. (D) Fluorescence lifetime of GPTMS, curcumin saturated aqueous solution and 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS. The stability of fluorescence intensity of 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS solution (λex/λem = 377/504 nm) exposure to UV irradiation (E) and natural light (F).

Fluorescence microscope observation: the fluorescence image of (A) curcumin saturated aqueous solution, (B) 80 μL GPTMS in 2 mL water, and (C) 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS solution. Bright field (D) and fluorescence (E) image of 80 μL of GPTMS added to 0.5 μg/mL curcumin aqueous solution (2 mL). (F) UV–vis spectra of GPTMS (80 μL) in 2 mL of H2O (GPTMS + H2O), curcumin saturated aqueous solution (curcumin), GPTMS (80 μL) with 2 mL curcumin aqueous solution of different concentrations (1.8–14.4 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS + H2O).

(A) Selectivity of GPTMS for curcumin over other interferents (X) and the coexisting interference of them, as described in Section 2.6. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of curcumin aqueous solution of different concentrations (0–2.0 μg/mL, 2 mL) with 80 μL GPTMS. Insert: the enlarged fluorescence emission spectra of curcumin at lower concentrations (0–0.05 μg/mL). (C) The linear fitting results (0.2–2000 ng/mL).

Photographs of solid materials prepared from 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS with (A) starch matrix and (B) PVA matrix under natural light and UV light (λ = 365 nm) in a dark box. (C) The fluorescent emission spectrum of LED diode coated with neutral silicone resin with and without 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS. Insert: LED diode coated with neutral silicone resin with and without 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS with power on.
Quantitative results and comparison of detected curcumin in various food extracts by GPTMS fluorescence method and HPLC method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: F 0.05, 4, 4 = 6.39;P > 0.05 indicated that there was no significant difference.

head.ce:title: Turn on fluorescence detection of curcumin in food matrices by the novel fluorescence sensitizer

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, silane reagents were for the first time explored as the fluorescence sensitizer. They were demonstrated to have fluorescence sensitization effect on curcumin and 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) possessed the strongest effect. Thus, GPTMS was adopted as the novel fluorescence sensitizer to turn on the fluorescence of curcumin by more than two orders of magnitude for detection. In this way, curcumin could be determined with a linear range of 0.2–2000 ng/mL and an LOD of 0.067 ng/mL. The method was applicable to determine curcumin in several actual food samples, which had the good consistency with the high performance liquid chromatographic method, demonstrating the high accuracy of the proposed method. In addition, the curcumins sensitized by GPTMS could be cured under certain conditions and held the potential for solid fluorescence application. This study expanded the scope of fluorescence sensitizer to silane reagents, and provided the novel approach for fluorescence detection of curcumin and further to generate new solid fluorescence system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin is present in the rhizomes of turmeric plants such as Curcuma longa Rhizoma (turmeric), and has been extensively studied to exert a variety of biological and pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, anticancer, antioxidant, antihepatotoxic, hypolipidemic and neuroprotective properties, etc. [1,2]. Also, curcumin is recognized by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as the natural additive for food, cosmetics, dietary and herbal supplements [3]. However, some studies disclosed that excessive intake of curcumin would have a prooxidative effect on DNA and reduce adenosine triphosphate in cells, which eventually led to cell necrosis and damage to human health [4]. WHO recommended that the daily dosage of curcumin as a food additive was not more than 0.3 mg/kg [5]. In view of the wide applications of curcumin in various fields, it was of great significance to accurately quantify the content of curcumin in food and related products, to ensure that the daily intake of curcumin was within the reasonable range.

Up to now, various methods have been developed for the analysis of curcumin, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [7], gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [8], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [9], UV–vis spectrophotometry [10], nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [11], electrochemical method [12] and capillary electrophoresis [13]. These methods possessed high accuracy and sensitivity, but also inevitable some disadvantages like requiring expensive equipment, complex sample pretreatment, tedious operation procedures, and long analysis time, which were not conducive to economic, rapid, and accurate determination of curcumin [3]. Alternatively, the fluorescence method was appreciated for its simple, instantaneous, sensitive and cost-effective characteristics [ [14,15]]. As curcumin owned weak fluorescence, some fluorescent probes, especially nanoprobes, were put forward to detect curcumin directly or indirectly, e.g. carbon dots (CDs) [16,17], double emission (Gd, Eu)2O3-PEI@CDs complex [18], Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots [19], graphene quantum dots [20], lanthanide upconversion nanoparticles [21], silver-doped graphene carbon nitride nanosheets [22], urazole-gold nanoclusters [23], etc. Nevertheless, the preparation of the fluorescent probe was tedious but imperative in these procedures. Thus, the simple and convenient method for the on-site sensitive detection of curcumin is still appealing.

In addition to the fluorescent probe, the fluorescence sensitization was another strategy to enhance sensitivity, in which fluorescence of the target would be largely enhanced in light of the fluorescence sensitizer. Micelles based on surfactants [De et al., 2002] and host-guest inclusion complexes [24,25] were the two commonly-used fluorescence sensitizers. For example, mixed micelles formed by sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS) and cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) were demonstrated to significantly enhance the fluorescence of curcumin, and the limit of detection (LOD) could reach 0.017 ng/mL [26]. In this approach, the fluorescence sensitizer was a decisive factor for the sensitivity.

The silane reagents were claimed to improve interfacial interaction between molecules, aiming to have superior chemical or physical properties [27]. Interestingly, in the present study, silane reagents were found to act as a novel type of fluorescence sensitizer, and applied for the turn-on fluorescence detection of curcumin. Different silane reagents behaved different sensitization ability to turn on the fluorescence of curcumin, among which 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) exerted the strongest. Hence, GPTMS was selected as the fluorescence sensitizer to quantify curcumin in a variety of actual food samples. In addition, the GPTMS and curcumin could be introduced into starch, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and silicone resin to form solid fluorescent materials, which expanded the application of sensitized fluorescence system in solid fluorescence. This new idea was really encouraging since it put forward a fancy way for the silane reagents into new applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Chemicals and materials
ce:section.ce:para: All the silane reagents used in the experiment (as listed in Table S1) were obtained from the WD silicone Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China). Curcumin, BaCl2, CaCl2, CuCl2, KCl, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2, FeSO4·7H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, NiSO4, Hg(NO3)2, AlCl3·6H2O, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, CH3COONa, NaH2PO4·2H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, Na3PO4, Cu(NO3)2, acetic acid (HAcO), PVA, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetyl-phenylalanine, galactose, cholesterol, aspirin, ascorbic acid and 4-hydroxybenzoic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dopamine, caffeic acid, ferulic acid, glutathione (GSH), 5-hydroxytryptophan, d-tryptophan, L-lysine, l-tryptophan, cysteine, alanine, L-phenylalanine, methionine, tyrosine, histidine and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were obtained from the Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Rhein, chrysophanol and gallic acid were purchased from McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Cianidanol and chlorogenic acid were obtained from the Bide Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was prepared by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: A Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) were used for UV–vis and fluorescence measurement, respectively. The emission spectra of the LED light were measured by an FP-6500 fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco, Japan). The fluorescence photos were taken with a DSY 5000X fluorescence microscope (UOP, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence sensitization of curcumin by silane reagents
ce:section.ce:para: The curcumin stock solution (368.4 μg/mL) was prepared by dissolving curcumin in ethanol and was then diluted to 18 μg/mL directly with a specific silane reagent (as listed in Table S1). The fluorescence spectra of the curcumin in silane reagents were then scanned with both excitation and emission slit width of 10 nm (the same below). The maximum excitation (λmax ex) and emission wavelengths (λmax em) of the curcumin in each different silane reagents were recorded and tabulated in Table S1 and the fluorescence intensities were recorded in Fig. 1 .

In order to study the optical behavior of curcumin in the presence of GPTMS, a series of curcumin solutions (1.8, 3.6, 9, 18, 36, 90, 180, 360 μg/mL) directly using GPTMS as the diluent (denoted as curcumin + GPTMS) were prepared. Then, the fluorescence spectra of each solution were scanned, and the λmax ex and λmax em were obtained for the respective solutions. Thus, the fluorescence intensities of each solution at respective λmax ex and λmax em, i.e. 421/504 nm (1.8 μg/mL), 420/504 nm (3.6 μg/mL), 394/504 nm (9 μg/mL), 377/504 nm (18 μg/mL), 366/504 nm (36 μg/mL), 345/504 nm (90 μg/mL), 322/508 nm (180 μg/mL) and 318/513 nm (360 μg/mL), were recorded.

For the fluorescence stability study, the 18 μg/mL curcumin dissolved in GPTMS (denoted as 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS) was continuously irradiated (λ = 377 nm) for 120 min, and the fluorescence intensity (λmax ex = 377 nm, λmax em = 504 nm) was monitored to evaluate the fluorescence stability. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of the solution exposed to natural light over time was also monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of curcumin in water and GPTMS were measured by using quinine sulfate as a reference (QYQ was 54% at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4). Briefly, curcumin and quinine sulfate were diluted to six different concentrations by water or GPTMS and 0.1 M H2SO4, respectively, ensuring the UV absorbance of each solution at 360 nm less than 0.1. The linear equation of the integrated fluorescence area (λex = 360 nm) with respect to absorbance values was fitted, and the QY values were calculated according to formula (1):
(1) QYC = (QYQ kC ƞC 2)/(kQ ƞQ 2)
Where Q and C referred to quinine sulfate and curcumin, respectively; k represented the slope of the linear equation; ƞ was the refractive index of the solvent (ƞ of GPTMS, H2O and 0.1 M H2SO4 solution were 1.43, 1.33 and 1.33, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of curcumin using GPTMS as fluorescence sensitizer
ce:section.ce:para: In the procedure of GPTMS as the fluorescence sensitizer to determine curcumin, the volume of GPTMS (10–100 μL), incubation time (5–180 s), NaCl concentration (0–1 M) and pH values (2–12), were optimized to attain the optimal detection conditions, using F’/F0’ as criterion. The typical detection operation was carried out as below. A prescribed volume of GPTMS was mixed with 2 mL 0.5 μg/mL curcumin aqueous solution of specified pH and NaCl concentration by vortexing, incubating for the prescribed time, and then subjected to the fluorescence measurement (λex = 432 nm, λem = 504 nm) to obtain the fluorescence intensity (F′). Meanwhile, for the samples without curcumin, the fluorescence intensity was measured as F0’ at the identical detection conditions.

For the linearity study, the stock solution of curcumin was diluted with ultrapure water to the gradient desired concentrations (0.2–2000 ng/mL). The detection was performed at the optimal conditions, i.e. 80 μL GPTMS was added to 2 mL of curcumin aqueous solution of specific concentrations with agitation, and the fluorescence intensity of the solution was then measured (λex = 432 nm, λem = 504 nm).

Limit of quantitation (LOQ) was obtained by the lowest concentration of linearity, and LOD was calculated according to the following formula (2):
(2) LOD = 3σ/k
Where σ denoted standard deviation of the blank solution, and k represented the slope of the fitted curve.

To verify the accuracy of the proposed methodology, three different concentrations (i.e. 1, 10 and 100 ng/mL) of the curcumin were determined at the optimal condition by using the fitted curves and the recovery was obtained by the ratio of the determined concentration to the spiked concentration. The precision of the method was accessed by the intra-day relative standard deviations (RSD) and inter-day RSDs of the repetitive measurements.

To evaluate the selectivity of the GPTMS to fluorescence sensitize curcumin, 80 μL GPTMS was added to 2 mL individual interference substance aqueous solution, including ions of 25 μM (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Cd2+、Co2+, Cr2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, NO3 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, CH3COO−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 −, PO4 3−), amino acids of 10 μg/mL (5-hydroxytryptophan, d-tryptophan, L-phenylalanine, L-lysine, l-tryptophan, cysteine, alanine, methionine, tyrosine, histidine), biological endogenous molecules and drugs of 10 μg/mL (dopamine, BSA, GSH, ascorbic acid, galactose, cholesterol and aspirin) and polyphenols of 10 μg/mL (4-hydroxybenzoic acid, ferulic acid, caffeic acid, rhein, chrysophanol, gallic acid, cianidanol, chlorogenic acid). For the coexisting effect study, 80 μL GPTMS was added to 2 mL of the above individual interference substance aqueous solution with curcumin of 0.5 μg/mL. All the samples were subjected to fluorescence intensity measurement (λex = 432 nm, λem = 504 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of curcumin in genuine food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fourteen food samples containing curcumin (three kinds of turmeric powder, four kinds of curry powder, five kinds of ginger powder, turmeric solid drink and sobering turmeric granule) were purchased from local supermarket or online platforms (Wuhan, China). All samples were ground to fine powder. Afterwards, 0.1 g powder sample was weighed and added with 1 mL EtOH with sonication for 15 min, followed by centrifugation for 15 min (12,000 rmp/min). The obtained supernatants were filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane, and the filtrate was divided into two parts. One part of the filtrate was diluted with ultrapure water to the detection concentration linear range by using GPTMS as the fluorescence sensitizer. Afterwards, 80 μL GPTMS was added to 2 mL of the treated sample solution, and the solution was then subjected to the fluorescence measurement (λex = 432 nm, λem = 504 nm). The content of curcumin in actual samples was calculated according to the linearity established in Section 2.5. For recovery study, curcuminoids were spiked to the above treated food samples and another four polyphenol-rich samples containing no curcumin (i.e. strawberry, apple, kiwifruit peel and tomato) to low (0.01 μg/mL), medium (0.1 μg/mL) and high (1.0 μg/mL) concentrations. The recovery (%) was the ratio of the concentration of curcumin actually detected to the sum of the spiked concentration and the actual original concentration in the sample.

The other part of the filtrate was subjected to the HPLC analysis to evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method. A Dionex 3000 chromatographic system (California, USA) with an ODS column (2.1 mm × 150 mm, 3 μm) was used for HPLC analysis. . Acetonitrile and 5% HAcO aqueous solution (35: 65, v: v) were the mobile phase with a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min, the injection volume was 5 μL and the UV detection wavelength was fixed at 420 nm for quantification [28]. Standard curcumin aqueous solution of different concentrations (i.e. 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 μg/mL) were injected to the HPLC for analysis. The standard calibration curve between the concentration of curcumin (5.0–25.0 μg/mL) and the total integrated area of chromatographic peaks by HPLC was thus established. The extracts of actual food samples were diluted to conform to the linear range concentration by HPLC analysis. The results obtained by GPTMS fluorescence method and HPLC method were analyzed by SPSS statistics 25 [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid-state fluorescence of curcumin sensitized by GPTMS
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate whether the fluorescence of curcumin sensitized by GPTMS could maintain in solid matrix or not, curcumin + GPTMS was introduced into different solid substrates. For starch substrate, 1 mL 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS was mixed with starch, which was directly placed in a 75 °C oven for drying, or put into the mold for molding and drying, and were then observed in a UV dark box (λ = 365 nm). To have an in-depth understanding of the solid fluorescence behavior of curcumin + GPTMS, curcumin of different concentrations + GPTMS were also investigated to mix with starch, i.e. 1.8, 9, 180, 360 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS. For PVA substrate, 1 g PVA (MW = 600) was dispersed in 8 mL H2O with ultrasonication for 20 min, and stirred at 95 °C until clarification. After that, 1 mL 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS was dispersed to the above PVA solution with stirring [30], which was molded to be solidified at room temperature and de-molded for observation. For silicone resin, 1 mL 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS was mixed well with 1 mL neutral silicone resin, into which an LED light-emitting diode (λex = 365 nm) was immersed and cured at 90 °C. The operation was repeated until the light-emitting diode was completely covered by the silicone resin. Thus-treated LED diode was connected to a power source in the dark to observe the luminescence, and the emission spectrum of it was recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer. The LED without the silicone resin was used as the blank.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussions
Sensitized fluorescence of curcumin by various silane reagents
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of the curcumin in different kinds of silane reagents were studied as described in Section 2.3. The λmax ex and λmax em of the curcumin in each silane reagents were listed in Table S1, and the fluorescence intensities at respective λmax em and λmax ex were recorded in Fig. 1. Both the studied silane reagents and curcumin possessed ignorable fluorescence themselves. However, remarkable turn-on fluorescence occurred once curcumin was dissolved in all the studied silane reagents. This observation demonstrated that the studied silane reagents had fluorescence sensitization effect on curcumin. Curcumin is a type of hydrophobic polyphenol compounds [31], which is difficult to dissolve in water, but easy to dissolve in some organic solvents like ethanol, methanol, acetone and chloroform. The studied silane reagents could provide hydrophobic microenvironment, thus enhancing the solubility of curcumin and resulting in a homogeneous and transparent solution as photographed in the inset of Fig. 1; meanwhile, the non-radiative emission of curcumin may be reduced [32], and thus the bright fluorescence was greatly strengthened.

Additionally, the silane reagents exhibited different fluorescence sensitization effects on the curcumin. In the case of N-methylaminopropyltrimethoxysilane and chlorotrimethylsilane, red color appeared visually and also the faint dark red fluorescence took place; for 3-chloropropyltriethoxysilane, GPTMS and 3-mercaptopropyltriethoxysilane, yellow fluorescence occurred; and for other studied silane agents, green fluorescence appeared. These phenomena may be attributed to the fact that the silane reagents possessed varied protonation or deprotonation ability, thus exhibiting different acid-base behaviors. As known, curcumin could act as an acid-base indicator with the color switch pH range of 7.8–9.2 As a result, different colors of curcumin were observed in different silane solutions under natural light [33]. In addition, the enhancement of fluorescence intensity differed in the presence of different silane reagents. The fluorescence intensity of curcumin in the corresponding 1–9 silane reagents in Fig. 1 was enhanced by about 348, 330, 294, 248, 239, 227, 130, 50 and 22 times, respectively, in the reference to curcumin itself in pure water. The above phenomenon demonstrated that the studied silane reagents possessed the ability to sensitize the fluorescence of curcumin. In addition, clearly, the silane reagent 1 (GPTMS) had the lowest fluorescence background and the highest turn on fluorescence, which was expected to ensure high detection sensitive. Hence, GPTMS was selected for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of curcumin in GPTMS
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of curcumin in GPTMS (curcumin + GPTMS) strengthened firstly with the elevated concentration of the curcumin, while weakened with further increasing concentration up to >18 μg/mL, which may be due to fluorescence self-quenching effect at the high concentration of curcumin [34]. The normalized fluorescence spectra corresponding to the curcumin of different concentrations in GPTMS were depicted in Fig. 2B. With the increasing concentration of the curcumin, the λmax ex blue-shifted obviously from 421 nm to 318 nm, while the λmax em only red-shifted slightly (about 9 nm) which finally reached 504 nm, probably due to the change of the surrounding microenvironment [35]. As the maximum fluorescence was obtained in the case of 18 μg/mL curcumin in GPTMS, this concentration was utilized for further experiments.

Fig. 2C showed that 18 μg/mL curcumin + GPTMS had bright green fluorescence (λ=365 nm). The QY values of curcumin were measured to be 0.11% in water and 11.1% in GPTMS (about 100 times enhancement of QY). The fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 2D) of curcumin saturated aqueous solution, GPTMS, and curcumin + GPTMS were 1.64 ns, 2.09 ns and 6.85 ns, respectively, further indicating that GPTMS significantly enhanced the fluorescence of the curcumin. As known, curcumin was vulnerable to the light, which greatly restricted its applicability [36]. However, the fluorescence of curcumin + GPTMS remained almost constant after being irradiated for 120 min (Fig. 2E). Even after being exposed to natural light for 60 days, the fluorescence intensity of curcumin + GPTMS still maintained more than 90% of the initial value (Fig. 2F). This phenomenon illustrated that GPTMS facilitated to enhance the optical stability of curcumin, and curcumin + GPTMS had good photobleaching resistance and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence sensitization of curcumin by GPTMS
ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin saturated aqueous solution (Fig. 3 A) and GPTMS aqueous solution (Fig. 3B) had almost ignorable fluorescence themselves, while curcumin + GPTMS exhibited uniform and bright green fluorescence (Fig. 3C). When curcumin + GPTMS were dispersed to the aqueous, as exhibited in Fig. 3D and E, green fluorescent spherical oily droplets were observed. GPTMS was hydrophobic in nature, and the curcumin was supposed to be easily soluble in GPTMS than in water as discussed above in Section 3.1. Once GPTMS was added to the aqueous, oily droplets were formed because of the hydrophobicity of GPTMS. Meanwhile, as the curcumin was more readily soluble in GPTMS, they would tend to be enriched in these oily GPTMS droplets. As a result, the polar microenvironment around the curcumin changed to less polar one, turning on the bright green fluorescence.

To further verify the mechanism, GPTMS was added to aqueous solutions with curcumin of different concentrations for UV–vis spectra measurement. As shown in Fig. 3F, the curcumin presented obvious absorption peak at about 420 nm and GPTMS presented at around 260 nm. Once the curcumin was present in GPTMS, the absorptions still remained respective to each other, possibly demonstrating no formation of new products between them. Thus, the fluorescence variance of the curcumin in GPTMS could not be ascribed to the formation of new products.

Moreover, as reported previously, some surfactants were claimed to bind with curcumin, forming micelles and causing fluorescence enhancement. The length and saturation of the alkyl chains of surfactants on the binding with the curcumin was significant. The curcumin would be solubilized in micelles so that the non-polar alkyl chains of the surfactants interacted with the non-polar aryl groups of the curcumin, while the longer and more saturated alkyl chains would favor the binding between the surfactant and the curcumin [37–39]. In our case, the saline reagents acted as the fluorescence sensitizers and they had difference in length and saturation in the alkyl chains. As listed in Table S1, for saline reagents 1, 2 and 3, there were about 7–8 atoms in the longest side chains of them, therefore, these chains could provide the more hydrophobic environment for the curcumin than other studied saline reagents. But the heterocyclic ring in the side chain of reagent 1 provided more hydrophobic environment than reagent 2, and meanwhile the reagent 3 was unsaturated compared with reagents 1 and 2. As a result, the degree of fluorescence enhancement of reagent 1 (GPTMS) was the strongest. For saline reagents 4–7, only 2–4 atoms were present at the side chains and hence the hydrophobicity provided by these reagents was lower than that of GPTMS. The reagent 8 has the hydrophilic group of –NH2 and reagent 9 has the very short side chains of –CH3 and -Cl, which hindered the formation of the preferable hydrophobic environment for curcumin, resulting in the weakest turn-on fluorescence among the nine studied silane reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of the curcumin using GPTMS as the fluorescence sensitizer
ce:section.ce:para: When GPTMS was added to the aqueous solution of the curcumin, the λmax ex and λmax em were different from those of curcumin + GPTMS system. The maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained at λmax ex/λmax em = 432/504 nm (Fig. S1) by adding 80 μL GPTMS to 2 mL curcumin aqueous solution. Therefore, the quantification of the curcumin was carried out at these wavelengths.

The conditions for GPTMS detection of the curcumin were optimized and shown in Fig. S2. The optimal response of F’/F0’ was obtained when 80 μL GPTMS (2 mL) was added (Fig. S2A). The strong basic conditions were unfavorable to the detection (Fig. S2B), which may be due to the fact that both GPTMS and curcumin were extremely unstable at basic conditions [ [40]]. On the other hand, the incubation time (Fig. S2C) and NaCl concentration (Fig. S2D) exerted almost no obvious effects on the detection of the curcumin by GPTMS. To be operational convenience, only the amount of GPTMS should be controlled for detection.

Method selectivity was crucial to the applicability of the fluorescence method. As shown in Fig. 4 A, possible potential interfering substances, e.g. metal ions, amino acids, biological endogenous substances, drugs and polyphenols, had little effect on the fluorescence of the curcumin sensitized by GPTMS. Also, the coexisting experimental results illustrated that the presence of the interfering substances exerted no significant influence on the fluorescence of the curcumin by GPTMS. Both the above results indicated the high selectivity of GPTMS as the fluorescence sensitizer to the curcumin, which was beneficial to the application of the method to actual samples.

The response of GPTMS to the curcumin of different concentrations in water was investigated under optimal detection conditions. The fluorescence intensity of solutions enhanced with the increasing concentration of the curcumin (Fig. 4B). A good linear relationship between the concentration of the curcumin with F/F0 in the range of 0.2–2000 ng/mL was obtained with the linear equation (Fig. 4C) of y = 240.44x+8.2262 and a correlation coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9957. The LOD was calculated to be 0.067 ng/mL and the LOQ was 0.2 ng/mL. By using the HPLC method, the standard calibration curve was established as y = 1.8975x-0.8593 with R2 of 0.9808 (Fig. S3). For intra- and inter-day precision and recovery are listed in Table S2. Both the inter-day and intra-day RSDs were less than 15%, indicating the precision of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of curcumin in genuine food samples using GPTMS as the fluorescence sensitizer
Comparison of detection methods for curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: The comparison was further made between some reported fluorescence methods and the present developed GPTMS method for quantifying curcumin in Table S4. Firstly, multi-step synthesis may be required for some fluorescent probes such as CDs [41], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [Du et al., 2020] etc. GPTMS was commercially available and directly used as a fluorescence sensitizer without any other treatment, which was of great convenience. Secondly, GPTMS showed higher selectivity and anti-interference ability by turning on the fluorescence of curcumin. Compared with the fluorescence quenching mode, the selectivity of turn-on fluorescence could be much better. In addition, GPTMS fluorescence method had similar or wider linear range and lower LOD (0.067 ng/mL) than the reported fluorescence methods [26], and was applicable to accurately determine the content of curcumin in a variety of food samples. Finally, herein, GPTMS was explored as a novel fluorescence sensitizer, in light of its hydrophobicity and functionality, to accurately quantify the curcumin by enriching them and turning on the fluorescence of them, which broadened the range of fluorescence sensitizers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid-state fluorescence of curcumin sensitized by GPTMS
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent materials with excellent optical properties were mostly applied in the liquid phase as severe fluorescence self-quenching may occur in solid state, which limited their applications such as LED light emitting materials and solid-state optoelectronic devices [42,43]. At present, the anti-self-quenching method to generate solid fluorescent materilas was mainly to introduce fluorescent materials into solid matrices, like starch [44], silica [45], polyvinylpyrrolidone [5], inorganic salts [46], etc., which increased the distance between the fluorescent materials and avoided fluorescence quenching by dispersing the fluorescent materials evenly in the solid matrix [47]. Considering the strong and stable fluorescence of curcumin sensitized by GPTMS, the applicability of this fluorescent sensitized system to the solid-state fluorescence was tested. As described in Section 2.8, the obtained solid materials were yellow-green under natural light (Fig. 5 A) and green fluorescent in the UV dark box (λ = 365 nm) (Fig. 5B). In addition, when cucurmin of different concentrations + GPTMS were mixed with starch, it showed the parabolic trend of curcumin concentration-dependent solid-state fluorescence changes (Fig. S5), which was well consistent with the observation in Fig. 2A. The fluorescence spectra of LED light (Fig. 5C) was scanned with the excitation power of the fluorometer off, which was consistent with the fluorescence in the solution (Fig. 2C). The blank LED diode emitted blue light, while the prepared LED diode emitted bright green light (λex = 365 nm), whose fluorescence intensity was much stronger than that of the blank group, when connected to the power supply as photographed in the insert of Fig. 5C. From this observation, curcumin sensitized by GPTMS could be solidified in the tested several substrates without obvious fluorescence quenching, and exhibited the potential to be used in solid fluorescence field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: For the first time, the fluorescence sensitization effect of different kinds of silane reagents on curcumin was investigated. Based on turning on the green fluorescence of curcumin, GPTMS could sensitively and accurately determine the content of curcumin in water with the LOD of 0.067 ng/mL. The preciseness and accuracy was also demonstrated in the application of the developed method to real food samples, with the superiority to the HPLC method. Besides, the application potential of curcumin sensitized by GPTMS to the solid-state fluorescence field was preliminarily explored. Moreover, the virtues of simple manufacturing process and stability owned by the silane agents may exceed other fluorescent materials, including the traditional quantum dots or surfactant which were toxic. These applications enlightened us to pay attention to the new property of the silane reagents and strike out a new research direction. In conclusion, a novel silane fluorescence sensitizer was proposed in the study, which provided a new strategy for quantitation of curcumin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Ya-Qian Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Li Li: Conceptualization, Methodology. Jin Yin: Discussion, Writing – review & editing. Xu Yu: Discussion, Writing – review & editing. Xiaowei Wu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Li Xu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering (SKLBEE2021014). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.
